<findings>

[observation] [warning] Coupling of 0.5 is at the warning threshold; the zone's tight relationship with web-dashboard-core (6 incoming imports) suggests it may be a candidate for absorption into the core zone rather than remaining separate.
[observation] [info] Including a ws-health-integration.test.ts and a boundary-check.test.ts alongside unit tests shows good layered testing strategy for real-time infrastructure.
[relationship] [info] Purely unidirectional dependency flow (consumed by web-dashboard-core, emits nothing outward) — reference pattern for how satellite zones should relate to the core zone.
[anti-pattern] [warning] boundary-check.test.ts enforces a package-wide server/viewer import boundary for all of src/ but is grouped into the websocket-infrastructure zone, making it appear to be a WebSocket contract test. This misplacement means the boundary enforcement test is invisible when reviewing the web package's overall test strategy and would not be found by developers looking for architectural guard tests.
[suggestion] [info] Move ws-health-integration.test.ts from tests/unit/server/ to tests/integration/server/ to match its name and actual testing tier; leaving it under unit/ makes CI tier filtering incorrect and misleads reviewers about the test's scope.
[suggestion] [info] Rename websocket.ts to ws-server.ts to match the ws-* prefix convention already used by ws-health-tracker.ts and ws-health-integration.test.ts within the same zone.

</findings>

<insights>

- Equal cohesion and coupling (0.5/0.5) places this zone right at the threshold — the WebSocket server and health tracker are related but health tracking could reasonably live in the core zone.
- Six files for two services with four test files demonstrates thorough test coverage including an integration test and a boundary check, which is a healthy pattern for infrastructure code.
- The zone is imported by web-dashboard-core (6 imports), making it a leaf dependency of the main dashboard; the dependency direction is clean.
- Coupling of 0.5 is at the warning threshold; the zone's tight relationship with web-dashboard-core (6 incoming imports) suggests it may be a candidate for absorption into the core zone rather than remaining separate.
- Including a ws-health-integration.test.ts and a boundary-check.test.ts alongside unit tests shows good layered testing strategy for real-time infrastructure.
- websocket-infrastructure is the only non-trivial zone in this batch with a strictly unidirectional dependency (web-viewer imports it; it imports nothing back), making it the healthiest architectural boundary among the satellite zones.
- Purely unidirectional dependency flow (consumed by web-dashboard-core, emits nothing outward) — reference pattern for how satellite zones should relate to the core zone.
- boundary-check.test.ts in this zone is not a WebSocket-specific test — it is a general server/viewer boundary enforcement test that scans all TypeScript files in src/ to detect any cross-boundary imports. Its placement in the websocket-infrastructure zone is a misclassification; it belongs in a root-level or integration test directory since it enforces a constraint on the entire web package, not just WebSocket code.
- boundary-check.test.ts enforces a package-wide server/viewer import boundary for all of src/ but is grouped into the websocket-infrastructure zone, making it appear to be a WebSocket contract test. This misplacement means the boundary enforcement test is invisible when reviewing the web package's overall test strategy and would not be found by developers looking for architectural guard tests.
- websocket.ts uses the full word while ws-health-tracker.ts uses the abbreviated 'ws-' prefix — inconsistent naming within the same zone; websocket.ts should be renamed ws-server.ts or ws-broker.ts to match the zone's prevailing convention.
- ws-health-integration.test.ts is placed in tests/unit/server/ despite its name explicitly signalling integration-test tier; placing an integration test under a unit/ directory creates ambiguity about the testing contract and makes CI tier filtering unreliable.
- The zone's coupling score of 0.5 is partly an artifact of boundary-check.test.ts, which imports across the entire src/ tree to scan for boundary violations — a file with package-wide import scope inflates the zone's coupling metric beyond what its two production files actually incur.
- Rename websocket.ts to ws-server.ts to match the ws-* prefix convention already used by ws-health-tracker.ts and ws-health-integration.test.ts within the same zone.
- Move ws-health-integration.test.ts from tests/unit/server/ to tests/integration/server/ to match its name and actual testing tier; leaving it under unit/ makes CI tier filtering incorrect and misleads reviewers about the test's scope.
- [call graph] 76 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 1 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
